title: Learning Lab — the prediction service we just built
subtitle: A worked, interactive walkthrough of one run. This is what the teaching-assistant generates after a build.

modules.title: This run: a prediction API
modules.summary: Understand and own what was built.

lessons.title: What we built, and why it is shaped this way
lessons.subtitle: In one breath, then the pieces.

blocks.title: In one breath
blocks.html: We built a small web service that takes a request, runs your model, and returns a prediction, packaged so it runs the same on any machine.
blocks.why: If you can say this sentence back, you understand the point of the whole build.

nodes.label: User
nodes.desc: Anything that sends an HTTP request: a browser, a script, another service.

nodes.label: API (FastAPI)
nodes.desc: The front door. Receives the request, validates it, and returns JSON. FastAPI gives typed inputs and free docs.

nodes.label: Service logic
nodes.desc: The code that prepares the input and calls the model. The part you will most often change.

nodes.label: Model
nodes.desc: The trained model loaded once at startup and reused for every request.

nodes.label: Database
nodes.desc: Stores requests and results so you can audit and improve later.

cards.front: What does the API layer do?
cards.back: Receives and validates requests, returns JSON responses. The front door.
Once at startup, then reused for every request, so it is fast.
So it runs the same on your laptop, a server, or a teammate's machine.

blocks.q: A request comes in. What touches it first?
blocks.explain: The API layer is the front door. It validates the request before anything else runs, then hands off to the service logic.

blocks.prompt: Explain, to a teammate, what happens from the moment a request arrives to the moment a prediction comes back.
blocks.model: The API layer receives and validates the request, passes it to the service logic, which prepares the input and calls the model loaded in memory. The model returns a prediction, the service optionally logs it to the database, and the API sends the result back as JSON.

lessons.title: Make it yours: try a change
lessons.subtitle: Learning by doing, the safe first edit.

blocks.title: The decisions, and what we traded
blocks.html:
ChoiceWhyTraded away
FastAPIFast to write, typed, free docsSlightly less mature than Flask for some plugins
Loaded model at startupFast responsesUses memory even when idle
DockerRuns the same everywhereOne more tool to learn

blocks.title: Add a health check
steps: Open the API file where routes are defined.
Add a new route '/health' that returns {'status':'ok'}.
Notice it flows through the same front door as every other request, just without touching the model.

yourturn.prompt: Add a '/version' endpoint that returns the model version string. Where does it go, and what does it touch?
yourturn.hint: It is a route on the API layer, like /health, but it reads one value from the service logic.
yourturn.solution: Add a route '/version' on the API layer that returns the version the service exposes (for example the model's version attribute). It touches the API layer and the service logic, but not the model's prediction path or the database. Same front door, smaller journey.

blocks.prompt: From memory: name the five pieces in the diagram and what each one does.
blocks.answer: User (sends the request), API layer (front door, validates, returns JSON), service logic (prepares input, calls the model), model (makes the prediction), database (stores requests and results for audit).

quiz.q: What is the API layer's job?
quiz.explain: It is the front door: validate in, JSON out.

quiz.q: Why is the model loaded at startup?
quiz.explain: Loading once and reusing keeps responses fast.
It is a route on the API layer and need not touch the model.
